channel.title: Privacy on RaihnForge
channel.description: Recent content in Privacy on RaihnForge

item.title: Can Closed Windows Be Open-Sourced?
item.description: Operating systems are no longer just helpful foundations for computers to be more functional. They are the digital landscapes many of us work, play, craft, and socialize inside on a daily basis. In my own case, working remotely with graphic design hobbies and keeping in touch with Veteran friends across the country, more of my time is spent in the OS environment than anywhere else. So what does it mean for individual privacy and liberty when this digital environment begins to aggressively exploit its user base’s attention, time, and personal data for profit? Windows 11 is the next iteration of the most prevalent operating system, and its implementation raises real concerns about individual privacy. What alternative solutions exist, and can they be viable against Microsoft’s goliath?

item.title: Spokeo v. Robins: Data Broker Privacy and Concrete Harm
item.description: Using Lessig’s four basic elements, Market, Law, Architecture, and Norms, this analysis seeks to better understand the regulatory effects and modality influences, both direct and indirect, that the case of Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins may have on personal liberty and ethics. The analysis reveals a potential critical disjoint in founding constitutional processes when addressing contemporary cybersecurity and privacy concerns. While the constitutional reasoning that allowed the Supreme Court to overturn the lower court’s initial finding in favor of Robins is clearly founded in precedent, it can be argued that “concrete evidence” of harm is not as easily obtained through the virtualization of persona and the datafication of people within cyberspace. Translation of ambiguous situations is required in order to accurately frame contemporary complexity.
